moveAnnotations.annotation: We're entering the Hungarian Opening, a quiet and flexible system that prepares a kingside fianchetto setup. Rather than contesting central control immediately, White plays a patient long-term game—we'll develop our bishop to g2 where it controls the long diagonal and exerts pressure on Black's position. This move gives us space on the kingside and keeps our options open about where we'll establish our center.

Black immediately challenges our kingside setup by attacking our h4 square before we can push pawns there. This aggressive counterplay is typical of Black's plan in such quiet openings—why wait passively when you can create immediate threats? By advancing the h-pawn, Black aims to weaken our kingside and potentially create pawn breaks or weaken our fianchetto.

We develop our knight while maintaining flexibility and controlling central squares like e5 and d4. The knight on f3 is ideally placed to support our fianchetto setup and prepares e4 if needed, giving us a solid foundation. This move keeps our position intact while we continue developing calmly—we're not rushing to respond to Black's h-pawn aggression.

Black persists with aggressive play, pushing the h-pawn further to attack g3 and create weakness around our king. This move signals that Black wants no part of a peaceful middlegame and is willing to accept a strange pawn structure for the sake of creating tactical opportunities. We must now decide whether to accept the damaged kingside or find ways to consolidate and turn the tables with our superior piece placement.
